**Edward Irving Koch** ([/kɒtʃ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KOTCH*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1) December 12, 1924 – February 1, 2013) was an American politician who served in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") from 1969 to 1977 and was [mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City") from 1978 to 1989. A popular figure, Koch rode the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") and stood at street corners greeting passersby with the slogan "How'm I doin'?"[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Koch was a lifelong [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") who described himself as a "liberal with sanity".[[3]](#cite_note-3) The author of an ambitious public housing renewal program in his later years as mayor, he began by cutting spending and taxes and cutting 7,000 employees from the city payroll. He was the city's second [Jewish](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City") mayor after his predecessor [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame").[[a]](#cite_note-6) He crossed party lines to endorse [Rudy Giuliani](/wiki/Rudy_Giuliani "Rudy Giuliani") for mayor of New York City in 1993, [Al D'Amato](/wiki/Al_D%27Amato "Al D'Amato") for Senate in 1998, [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") for mayor of New York City in 2001, and [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") for president in 2004.[[6]](#cite_note-NYSunObit-7)

Koch was first elected mayor of New York City in [1977](/wiki/1977_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1977 New York City mayoral election") and was re-elected in [1981](/wiki/1981_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1981 New York City mayoral election") with 75% of the vote. He was the first New York City mayor to win endorsement on both the Democratic and Republican party tickets. In [1985](/wiki/1985_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1985 New York City mayoral election"), Koch was elected to a third term with 78% of the vote. His third term was fraught with scandal regarding political associates (although the scandal never touched him personally) and with racial tensions, including the killings of [Michael Griffith](/wiki/Howard_Beach_incident "Howard Beach incident") and [Yusuf Hawkins](/wiki/Yusuf_Hawkins "Yusuf Hawkins"). In a close race, Koch lost the [1989](/wiki/1989_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1989 New York City mayoral election") Democratic primary to his successor, [David Dinkins](/wiki/David_Dinkins "David Dinkins").[[6]](#cite_note-NYSunObit-7)

Koch was a lifelong bachelor. Though he publicly denied being [gay](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") on numerous occasions, he attempted to find a boyfriend late in life.[[7]](#cite_note-NYT22-8)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Koch was born in the [Crotona Park East](/wiki/Crotona_Park_East "Crotona Park East") section of [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") in New York City,[[8]](#cite_note-congress_bio-9) the son of Yetta (or Joyce,[[9]](#cite_note-Census-10) née Silpe) and Louis (Leib) Koch, [Polish-Jewish](/wiki/Polish-Jewish "Polish-Jewish") immigrants from [Kozliv](/wiki/Kozliv "Kozliv") and Uścieczko ([Ustechko](/wiki/Ustechko,_Chortkiv_Raion,_Ternopil_Oblast "Ustechko, Chortkiv Raion, Ternopil Oblast")) in [Eastern Galicia](/wiki/Eastern_Galicia "Eastern Galicia"), both located in [Ternopil Oblast](/wiki/Ternopil_Oblast "Ternopil Oblast") in western [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").[[10]](#cite_note-11) He came from a family of [Conservative Jews](/wiki/Conservative_Jewish "Conservative Jewish") who resided in [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), where his father worked at a theater. As a child, he worked as a [hatcheck](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hatcheck "wiktionary:hatcheck") boy in a Newark dance hall.[[11]](#cite_note-12) He graduated from [South Side High School](/wiki/Malcolm_X_Shabazz_High_School "Malcolm X Shabazz High School") in Newark in 1941.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: World War II")]

In 1943, Koch was drafted into the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army").[[13]](#cite_note-NARA_WWII_Enlistment_Records_–_Ed_Koch-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Koch did his basic training at [Camp Croft](/wiki/Camp_Croft,_South_Carolina "Camp Croft, South Carolina"), South Carolina, in 1943 before entering the [Army Specialized Training Program](/wiki/Army_Specialized_Training_Program "Army Specialized Training Program").[[15]](#cite_note-16) He then joined the [104th Infantry Division](/wiki/104th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "104th Infantry Division (United States)"). On 27 August 1944, he departed New York City, landing in [Cherbourg](/wiki/Cherbourg "Cherbourg"), France, on 7 September 1944. He earned a [European-African-Middle Eastern Campaign Medal](/wiki/European-African-Middle_Eastern_Campaign_Medal "European-African-Middle Eastern Campaign Medal") with two [campaign stars](/wiki/Campaign_stars "Campaign stars"), a [World War II Victory Medal](/wiki/World_War_II_Victory_Medal "World War II Victory Medal"), and the [Combat Infantryman Badge](/wiki/Combat_Infantryman_Badge "Combat Infantryman Badge") for service in the [European Theater of Operations](/wiki/European_Theater_of_Operations "European Theater of Operations"). After V-E Day, because he could speak German, Koch was sent to [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") to help remove Nazi public officials from their jobs and find non-Nazis to take their place. He was honorably discharged with the rank of sergeant in 1946.[[8]](#cite_note-congress_bio-9)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Post-World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-World War II")]

Koch returned to New York City to attend [City College of New York](/wiki/City_College_of_New_York "City College of New York"), graduating in 1945, and [New York University School of Law](/wiki/New_York_University_School_of_Law "New York University School of Law"), receiving his law degree in 1948. Koch was a [sole practitioner](/wiki/Sole_practitioner "Sole practitioner") from 1949 to 1964, and a partner with Koch, Lankenau, Schwartz & Kovner from 1965 to 1968. A [Democrat](/wiki/New_York_Democratic_Party "New York Democratic Party"), he became active in New York City politics as a reformer and opponent of [Carmine DeSapio](/wiki/Carmine_DeSapio "Carmine DeSapio") and [Tammany Hall](/wiki/Tammany_Hall "Tammany Hall"). In 1962 Koch ran for office for the first time, unsuccessfully opposing incumbent William Passannante, a DeSapio ally, for the Democratic nomination for the [State Assembly](/wiki/New_York_State_Assembly "New York State Assembly").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

In 1963, Koch defeated DeSapio for the position of Democratic Party leader for the district which included [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), and Koch won again in a 1965 rematch.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Koch served on the [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") from 1967 to 1969.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Career")]

### Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Elections")]

#### 1968

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1968")]

Koch ran for Congress in [New York's 17th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_17th_congressional_district "New York's 17th congressional district") after Republican [Theodore Roosevelt Kupferman](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Kupferman "Theodore Roosevelt Kupferman") retired. He defeated Republican [Whitney Seymour Jr.](/wiki/Whitney_Seymour_Jr. "Whitney Seymour Jr.") and Conservative Richard J. Callahan, who partly split the conservative vote.[[20]](#cite_note-21) He won 48.5% of the vote to Seymour's 45.6% and Callahan's 5.9%.

#### 1970

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1970")]

Koch was reelected with 62% of the vote, defeating Republican Peter J. Sprague and Conservative Callahan who finished with 32% and 6%, respectively.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

#### 1972

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1972")]

In advance of the 1972 elections, Koch was redistricted into the [18th district](/wiki/New_York%27s_18th_congressional_district "New York's 18th congressional district"). He defeated Republican Jane Pickens Langley and Socialist Workers nominee Rebecca Finch, 70%–29%–1%.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

#### 1973

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1973")]

See also: [1973 New York City mayoral election](/wiki/1973_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1973 New York City mayoral election")

Koch briefly ran for mayor in 1973, but garnered little support and dropped out before the Democratic primary.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Comptroller [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame") won the election.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

#### 1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1974")]

Koch won reelection (with career-best 76.7% of the vote) to the 18th district against John Boogaerts Jr. (Republican, 18.8%), Gilliam M. Drummond (Conservative, 3.7%), and Katherine Sojourner ([Socialist Workers](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)"), 0.8%).[[25]](#cite_note-26)

#### 1976

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1976")]

Koch was again reelected, this time with 75.7% of the vote, defeating Sonia Landau (Republican, 20.1%), and James W. McConnell (Conservative, 4.3%).[[26]](#cite_note-27)

#### 1977

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1977")]

See also: [1977 New York City mayoral election](/wiki/1977_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1977 New York City mayoral election")

Koch announced his campaign for mayor of New York City against incumbent Beame. Koch and future governor [Mario Cuomo](/wiki/Mario_Cuomo "Mario Cuomo") finished first (19.8%) and second (18.7%) in the Democratic primary, eliminating Beame (18%). In a runoff, Koch defeated Cuomo, 55%-45%. In the general election, Koch again defeated Cuomo, who ran on the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_New_York "Liberal Party of New York") ticket, 50% to 41%.

Koch ran to the right of the other candidates on a "[law and order](/wiki/Law_and_order_(politics) "Law and order (politics)")" platform. According to historian Jonathan Mahler, the [New York City blackout of July 1977](/wiki/New_York_City_blackout_of_1977 "New York City blackout of 1977") and the subsequent rioting helped catapult Koch and his message of restoring public safety to front-runner status.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

#### 1981

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1981")]

See also: [1981 New York City mayoral election](/wiki/1981_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1981 New York City mayoral election")

Koch won both the Democratic and Republican nominations and appeared on the ballot with both of their lines. He faced opposition only from third parties. He won 74.6% of the vote, with Unity candidate [Frank Barbaro](/wiki/Frank_Barbaro "Frank Barbaro") netting next-best 13.3%. John A. Esposito (Conservative) and Mary T. Codd (Liberal) also ran.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Koch swept all five boroughs by landslide margins, breaking 60% of the vote in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and 70% in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

#### 1982

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1982")]

See also: [1982 New York gubernatorial election](/wiki/1982_New_York_gubernatorial_election "1982 New York gubernatorial election")

After incumbent [Hugh Carey](/wiki/Hugh_Carey "Hugh Carey") announced he would not run for reelection, Koch announced his candidacy for governor of New York. Cuomo, who had been elected lieutenant governor, also ran. Koch received the party's endorsement with 61% of the convention vote, but Cuomo won the Democratic primary. *The New York Times* called Cuomo's victory a "stunning upset" that relied on "an unusual coalition of liberal Democrats, labor, minorities and upstaters". Koch ran strongly in Jewish communities, while Cuomo won black, liberal, and Italian communities by a similar margin. A key to Cuomo's victory was his strong showing in New York City itself; though Koch won the city and its four suburban counties (Rockland, Westchester, Suffolk, and Nassau) as expected, Cuomo kept the margin close and won half of the city's Assembly districts. That, combined with large victories in nearly every upstate county, allowed Cuomo to win. Koch endorsed Cuomo immediately, declaring "what's important to all of us is that we keep a Democrat in Albany."[[30]](#cite_note-31) During the campaign, posters cropped up which mocked Koch's sexuality which written "Vote Cuomo not the Homo" circulated.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Many say the deciding factor in Koch's loss was an interview with *[Playboy](/wiki/Playboy "Playboy")* magazine in which he critiqued the lifestyle of suburbia. Koch's remarks are thought to have alienated many voters from outside New York City.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Cuomo was elected governor over Republican [Lewis Lehrman](/wiki/Lewis_Lehrman "Lewis Lehrman"), and served three terms.

#### 1985

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1985")]

See also: [1985 New York City mayoral election](/wiki/1985_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1985 New York City mayoral election")

Koch was reelected to a third term in a landslide. He defeated Carol Bellamy (Liberal) and Dian McGrath (Republican/Conservative), 78%-10%-9%, respectively, and was sworn into his third and final term in January 1986. As of 2021, this is the most recent mayoral election in which a Democrat carried Staten Island.[[33]](#cite_note-34) During the campaign, Koch visited the Lubavitcher Rabbi [Menachem Mendel Schneerson](/wiki/Menachem_Mendel_Schneerson "Menachem Mendel Schneerson"), seeking his blessing and endorsement.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

#### 1989

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1989")]

See also: [1989 New York City mayoral election](/wiki/1989_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1989 New York City mayoral election")

Koch ran for an unprecedented fourth term in 1989. No mayor had ever won a fourth term, though [Fiorello La Guardia](/wiki/Fiorello_La_Guardia "Fiorello La Guardia") and [Robert Wagner](/wiki/Robert_F._Wagner_Jr. "Robert F. Wagner Jr.") also served three terms, and Wagner attempted to run for a fourth in [1969](/wiki/1969_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1969 New York City mayoral election"). Koch lost the Democratic primary to Manhattan Borough President [David Dinkins](/wiki/David_Dinkins "David Dinkins"), who finished with 547,901 votes to Koch's 456,313. Dinkins was helped in part by large margins in Manhattan, the Bronx, and Brooklyn, while Koch carried Staten Island and Queens.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Dinkins defeated [Rudy Giuliani](/wiki/Rudy_Giuliani "Rudy Giuliani") in the general election by a narrow margin, with Giuliani carrying both counties Koch won in the primary. Giuliani won a rematch against Dinkins in [1993](/wiki/1993_New_York_City_mayoral_election "1993 New York City mayoral election").

### U.S. congressional tenure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: U.S. congressional tenure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Ed_Koch_95th_congress.jpg/250px-Ed_Koch_95th_congress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ed_Koch_95th_congress.jpg)

Koch served in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") from 1969 to 1977.

Koch was the Democratic U.S. Representative from [New York's 17th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_17th_congressional_district "New York's 17th congressional district") from January 3, 1969, until January 3, 1973, when, after a redistricting, he represented [New York's 18th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_18th_congressional_district "New York's 18th congressional district") until December 31, 1977, when he resigned to become Mayor of New York City.[[36]](#cite_note-columbia-37)

Koch said he began his political career as "just a plain liberal", with positions including opposing the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") and marching in the South for civil rights.[[37]](#cite_note-38) In April 1973, Koch coined the term "[Watergate Seven](/wiki/Watergate_Seven "Watergate Seven")" when, in response to U.S. Senator [Lowell P. Weicker Jr.](/wiki/Lowell_P._Weicker_Jr. "Lowell P. Weicker Jr.")'s indicating that one of the men in [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal") had been ordered in the spring of 1972 to keep certain senators and representatives under surveillance, he posted a sign on his office door reading, "These premises were surveilled by the Watergate Seven. Watch yourself".[[38]](#cite_note-39) At about the same time, Koch began his rightward shift toward being a "liberal with sanity" after reviewing the 1973 controversy around then-New York City Mayor [John Lindsay](/wiki/John_Lindsay "John Lindsay")'s attempt to place a 3,000-person [housing project](/wiki/Housing_project "Housing project") in a middle-class community in [Forest Hills, Queens](/wiki/Forest_Hills,_Queens "Forest Hills, Queens"). Koch met with residents of the community, most of whom were against the proposal. He was convinced by their arguments, and spoke out against the plan, shocking some of his liberal allies.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

Koch was active in advocating for a greater U.S. role in advancing human rights within the context of fighting Communism. He had particular influence in the foreign aid budget, as he sat on the [House Appropriations Subcommittee on Foreign Operations](/wiki/United_States_House_Appropriations_Subcommittee_on_State,_Foreign_Operations,_and_Related_Programs "United States House Appropriations Subcommittee on State, Foreign Operations, and Related Programs"). In 1976, Koch proposed that the U.S. cut off military aid and supplies to the [military dictatorship](/wiki/Civic-military_dictatorship_of_Uruguay "Civic-military dictatorship of Uruguay") of Uruguay. In mid-July 1976, the [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") learned that two high-level Uruguayan intelligence officers had discussed a possible assassination attempt on Koch by [Dirección de Inteligencia Nacional](/wiki/Direcci%C3%B3n_de_Inteligencia_Nacional "Dirección de Inteligencia Nacional") (DINA), the Chilean [secret police](/wiki/Secret_police "Secret police") under dictator [Augusto Pinochet](/wiki/Augusto_Pinochet "Augusto Pinochet"). The CIA did not regard these threats as credible until after the September 1976 [assassination of Orlando Letelier](/wiki/Assassination_of_Orlando_Letelier "Assassination of Orlando Letelier") in Washington, D.C., by DINA agents coordinated by [Operation Condor](/wiki/Operation_Condor "Operation Condor"). After that, [Director of Central Intelligence](/wiki/Director_of_Central_Intelligence "Director of Central Intelligence") [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") informed Koch of the threat. Koch subsequently asked both the CIA and the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_Investigation "Federal Bureau Investigation") for protection, but none was extended.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

### Tenure as mayor of New York City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tenure as mayor of New York City")]

#### First term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: First term")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/President_Ronald_Reagan_presenting_New_York_leaders_with_a_check_for_Westway_Project_Funds.jpg/250px-President_Ronald_Reagan_presenting_New_York_leaders_with_a_check_for_Westway_Project_Funds.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Ronald_Reagan_presenting_New_York_leaders_with_a_check_for_Westway_Project_Funds.jpg)

President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") presenting Ed Koch and other New York leaders, including Governor [Mario Cuomo](/wiki/Mario_Cuomo "Mario Cuomo"), with a check for Westway Project Funds, September 1981

When Koch entered office the city was facing multiple serious issues. The city was in financial crisis, crime rates were soaring, and the city was still recovering from a [major blackout](/wiki/New_York_City_blackout_of_1977 "New York City blackout of 1977") and looting. Koch instituted austerity measures which put the city on better financial foundations for his second term.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

During his first term as mayor, which many consider his best, a number of major events occurred in New York City. [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon")'s abrupt assassination sent shock waves around the world. Grief-stricken New Yorkers walked the streets openly crying for days after the shooting, which took place in front of [The Dakota](/wiki/The_Dakota "The Dakota"), Lennon's place of residence on Manhattan's Upper West Side.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Koch also dealt with the [second transit strike](/wiki/1980_New_York_City_transit_strike "1980 New York City transit strike"), and pushed for the 1980 Democratic National Convention to be in NYC.[[43]](#cite_note-44) His first term also saw a sister city relationship begin with Beijing.

#### Second term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Second term")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/New_York_mayor_Ed_Koch.jpg/250px-New_York_mayor_Ed_Koch.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_mayor_Ed_Koch.jpg)

Koch giving interview to [WABC-TV](/wiki/WABC-TV "WABC-TV") in 1981

Among the events of Koch's second term as mayor were the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge")'s 100th anniversary, the appointing of [Benjamin Ward](/wiki/Benjamin_Ward "Benjamin Ward") as the city's first ever African American [police commissioner](/wiki/Police_commissioner "Police commissioner") in 1983, the emergence of AIDS as a public health crisis, extensive media coverage of [Bernhard Goetz](/wiki/Bernhard_Goetz "Bernhard Goetz")'s shooting of four African American teenagers in the subway in 1984, and the United Nations' 40th anniversary.[[44]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Back_in_the_1980s-45)

Koch often deviated from the conventional liberal line, strongly supporting the death penalty, adding 3,500 officers to the NYPD in the 1980s,[[45]](#cite_note-46) and taking a hard line on "[quality of life](/wiki/Quality_of_life "Quality of life")" issues, such as giving police broader powers in dealing with the homeless and signing legislation banning the playing of radios on subways and buses. These positions prompted harsh criticism from the local chapter of the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union") and many African-American leaders, particularly Reverend [Al Sharpton](/wiki/Al_Sharpton "Al Sharpton").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

In 1984, Koch published his first memoir, *Mayor*, which became a best-seller and was adapted into an [off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway") and later [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") musical, *[Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_(musical) "Mayor (musical)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Third term

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Third term")]

In 1986, Koch signed a lesbian and gay rights ordinance for the city after the City Council passed the measure (on March 20), after 15 years of failed attempts by that body to approve such legislation. Despite his overall pro-lesbian and pro-gay-rights stance, he nonetheless backed up the [New York City Health Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Health_Department "New York City Health Department")'s decision to shut down the city's [gay bathhouses](/wiki/Gay_bathhouse "Gay bathhouse") in 1985 in response to concerns over the spread of AIDS. The enactment of the measure the next year placed the city in a dilemma, as it apparently meant that the bathhouses would have to be reopened because many heterosexual "sex clubs" – such as [Plato's Retreat](/wiki/Plato%27s_Retreat "Plato's Retreat") – were in operation in the city at the time, and allowing them to remain open while keeping the bathhouses shuttered would have been a violation of the newly adopted anti-discrimination law. The Health Department, with Koch's approval, reacted by ordering the heterosexual clubs, including Plato's Retreat, to close as well. Also in 1986, Koch participated in [Hands Across America](/wiki/Hands_Across_America "Hands Across America") and in the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty")'s 100th anniversary celebration. Koch's third term was also marked by the career-ending face-slashing of model [Marla Hanson](/wiki/Marla_Hanson "Marla Hanson"), the paralyzing shooting of NYCPD detective [Steven McDonald](/wiki/Steven_McDonald "Steven McDonald"), the [crack cocaine epidemic](/wiki/Crack_epidemic_in_the_United_States "Crack epidemic in the United States") and its related gangs, the [Robert Chambers](/wiki/Robert_Chambers_(criminal) "Robert Chambers (criminal)") "preppie murder" case, the [Howard Beach incident](/wiki/Howard_Beach_incident "Howard Beach incident"), and the racially motivated [murder of Yusef Hawkins](/wiki/Murder_of_Yusef_Hawkins "Murder of Yusef Hawkins").[[44]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Back_in_the_1980s-45)

Koch consistently demonstrated a fierce love for New York City, which some observers felt he carried to extremes on occasion: in 1984, he went on record as opposing the creation of a second telephone [area code](/wiki/Area_code "Area code") for the city, claiming that this would divide the city's population; and when the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") won [Super Bowl XXI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXI "Super Bowl XXI") in January 1987, he refused to grant a permit for the team to hold their traditional victory parade in the city, quipping famously, "If they want a parade, let them parade in front of the oil drums in [Moonachie](/wiki/Moonachie "Moonachie")" (a town in New Jersey adjacent to the [East Rutherford](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey") site of the [Meadowlands Sports Complex](/wiki/Meadowlands_Sports_Complex "Meadowlands Sports Complex"), where the Giants play their home games).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Edward_Koch_at_commissioning_of_USS_Lake_Champlain_%28CG-57%29_cropped.jpg/250px-Edward_Koch_at_commissioning_of_USS_Lake_Champlain_%28CG-57%29_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Koch_at_commissioning_of_USS_Lake_Champlain_(CG-57)_cropped.jpg)

Edward Koch at the commissioning of [USS *Lake Champlain*](/wiki/USS_Lake_Champlain_(CG-57) "USS Lake Champlain (CG-57)") (1988)

In his third term, Koch's popularity was shaken after a series of corruption scandals, touched off by [Donald Manes](/wiki/Donald_Manes "Donald Manes")'s suicide and the Parking Violations Bureau scandal, which revealed that he had acceded to the requests of political allies (most notably Queens Borough President Manes, Bronx Democratic Party official [Stanley M. Friedman](/wiki/Stanley_M._Friedman "Stanley M. Friedman") and Brooklyn Democratic Party chairman [Meade Esposito](/wiki/Meade_Esposito "Meade Esposito"), an [American Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia") associate long perceived as New York City's preeminent political leader) to stack city agencies with patronage appointments. There were no allegations that Koch obtained any financial benefit from the corruption, but the scandals undermined Koch's claims that he ran a patronage-free municipal government. Michael Tager attributes the scandals not to Koch's failures but to the steadily declining power of the Democratic machine and its bosses' desperate efforts to reverse the collapse.[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)

In July 1987, Koch proposed banning bicycling on [Fifth](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue"), [Park](/wiki/Park_Avenue "Park Avenue") and [Madison](/wiki/Madison_Avenue "Madison Avenue") avenues during weekdays, but many bicyclists protested and had the ban overturned.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Burgemeester_Edward_Koch_van_New_York_bezoekt_Amsterdam_burgemeester_Van_Thijn_%28%2C_Bestanddeelnr_934-2885.jpg/250px-Burgemeester_Edward_Koch_van_New_York_bezoekt_Amsterdam_burgemeester_Van_Thijn_%28%2C_Bestanddeelnr_934-2885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burgemeester_Edward_Koch_van_New_York_bezoekt_Amsterdam_burgemeester_Van_Thijn_(,_Bestanddeelnr_934-2885.jpg)

Koch with [Ed van Thijn](/wiki/Ed_van_Thijn "Ed van Thijn") on a visit to [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") in 1988

It has been said that race relations in Koch's last years in office were poor.[[51]](#cite_note-52) He became a controversial figure in the [1988 presidential campaign](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States)_presidential_primaries,_1988 "Democratic Party (United States) presidential primaries, 1988") with his public criticism of Democratic candidate [Jesse Jackson](/wiki/Jesse_Jackson_presidential_campaign,_1988 "Jesse Jackson presidential campaign, 1988"), who surprised many political observers by winning key primaries in March and running even with the front-runner, Massachusetts Governor [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis"). As the April New York primary approached, Koch reminded voters of Jackson's earlier [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") statements, and said that Jews would be "crazy" to vote for Jackson. Koch endorsed Tennessee Senator [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore_presidential_campaign,_1988 "Al Gore presidential campaign, 1988"), who had run well in his native South but hadn't won 20% in a northern state. As Koch's anti-Jackson rhetoric intensified, Gore seemed to shy away from Koch. On primary day, Gore finished a weak third with 10% of the vote and dropped out of the race. Jackson ran ten points behind Dukakis, whose nomination became assured after his New York win.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

#### Assessments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Assessments")]

A 1993 survey of historians, political scientists and urban experts conducted by Melvin G. Holli of the [University of Illinois at Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Chicago "University of Illinois at Chicago") ranked Koch as the 15th-worst American big-city mayor to have served between 1820 and 1993.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Other analyses rate his tenure more favorably.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In the final chapter of *Ed Koch and the Rebuilding of New York City* (Columbia University Press, 2010), NYU history professor Jonathan Soffer wrote: "Koch faced challenges greater than any New York mayor of the 20th century and met many of them." He added, "Koch bravely faced one of the worst crises in New York history, restructured the city with minimal help from the federal government and kept it solvent and growing for a generation." And Soffer concluded, "Koch's tireless personal lobbying campaign led to quite simply the greatest turnaround accomplished by any New York mayor in the twentieth century, including Fiorello La Guardia."[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In *[The New York Times Book Review](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Book_Review "The New York Times Book Review")*, [Sam Roberts](/wiki/Sam_Roberts_(newspaper_journalist) "Sam Roberts (newspaper journalist)"), former city editor for the New York *Daily News* and urban affairs correspondent for *The New York Times*, reconciled Holli's negative survey of 1993 with Soffer's analysis of 2010, writing, "Perhaps the survey was taken too soon, before Mr. Koch's legacy could be fully appreciated."[[56]](#cite_note-57)

### Post-mayoral years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Post-mayoral years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/2004_OSCE_Anti-Semitism_Conference_photo_op_cropped.jpg/250px-2004_OSCE_Anti-Semitism_Conference_photo_op_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:2004_OSCE_Anti-Semitism_Conference_photo_op_cropped.jpg)

Koch and [Colin Powell](/wiki/Colin_Powell "Colin Powell") lead the US delegation for the 2004 [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") Conference on Anti-Semitism, held in Berlin, Germany (April 28, 2004)

In the years following his mayoralty, Koch became a partner in the law firm of [Robinson, Silverman, Pearce, Aronsohn, and Berman](/wiki/Robinson,_Silverman,_Pearce,_Aronsohn,_and_Berman "Robinson, Silverman, Pearce, Aronsohn, and Berman") LLP (now [Bryan Cave](/wiki/Bryan_Cave "Bryan Cave") LLP) and a commentator on politics, as well as reviewing movies and restaurants for newspapers, radio and television. He also became an adjunct professor at [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") (NYU) and the judge on *[The People's Court](/wiki/The_People%27s_Court "The People's Court")* for two years (1997–99) following the retirement of [Judge Joseph Wapner](/wiki/Joseph_Wapner "Joseph Wapner"). In 1999, he was a visiting professor at [Brandeis University](/wiki/Brandeis_University "Brandeis University"). Koch regularly appeared on the lecture circuit, and had a high-rated talk show on [WABC](/wiki/WABC_(AM) "WABC (AM)") radio. He also hosted his own online movie review show, *The Mayor at the Movies*.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

A street in southern [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") was named after Koch in an August 12, 1993, ceremony attended by him alongside prominent Israeli and American dignitaries.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

In 2004, together with his sister Pat (also Pauline)[[9]](#cite_note-Census-10) Koch Thaler, Koch wrote a children's book, *Eddie, Harold's Little Brother*; it tells the story of Koch's childhood, when he tried unsuccessfully to emulate his older brother Harold's baseball talents, before realizing that he should instead focus on what he was already good at, which was telling stories and speaking in public.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

The [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") voted to rename the [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") the Ed Koch Queensboro Bridge on March 23, 2011.[[61]](#cite_note-KochBridge-62) Later, city councilman [Peter Vallone](/wiki/Peter_Vallone "Peter Vallone") introduced legislation banning the naming of New York City property after people who are still alive, but the legislation failed.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

Koch formed an organization called New York Uprising to push for statewide redistricting reform. In April 2011, he publicly upbraided 42 state legislators he claimed had broken their promises to support redistricting reform.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Koch_%E2%80%93_Mayor_of_the_City_of_New_York.jpg/250px-Koch_%E2%80%93_Mayor_of_the_City_of_New_York.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koch_%E2%80%93_Mayor_of_the_City_of_New_York.jpg)

Portrait of Koch by [Dmitry Borshch](/wiki/Dmitry_Borshch "Dmitry Borshch"), 2011

In May 2011, Koch sat for a portrait by [Dmitry Borshch](/wiki/Dmitry_Borshch "Dmitry Borshch") that has been exhibited at the [Institute of Oriental Studies of the Russian Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Institute_of_Oriental_Studies_of_the_Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Institute of Oriental Studies of the Russian Academy of Sciences"), [DePaul University](/wiki/DePaul_University "DePaul University"), [Brecht Forum](/wiki/Brecht_Forum "Brecht Forum"), and [CUNY Graduate Center](/wiki/The_Graduate_Center,_CUNY "The Graduate Center, CUNY"), and is included in the [Catalog of American Portraits](/wiki/Catalog_of_American_Portraits "Catalog of American Portraits") at the Smithsonian's [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery_(United_States) "National Portrait Gallery (United States)").[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)

#### "Mayor at the Movies"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: \"Mayor at the Movies\"")]

Koch began appearing in weekly movie review segments for an online show, *Mayor at the Movies*, in the summer of 2009.[[67]](#cite_note-mayorkoch.com-68) He was an avid moviegoer who often saw two or three movies a weekend. Although he was invited to private screenings, Koch preferred to see films with a public audience and was often approached by moviegoers who were surprised to find him there. His reviews were outspoken and wry, with his rating system consisting not of stars but of a "plus" for a good film or a "minus" for a bad one. He sought out great documentaries, and had a particular passion for anything of Jewish interest.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

He had a particular passion for independent cinema and documentaries, but enjoyed dramas and action films as well. In addition to *Mayor at the Movies*,[[67]](#cite_note-mayorkoch.com-68) his film reviews were regularly featured on *[The Huffington Post](/wiki/The_Huffington_Post "The Huffington Post")*[[69]](#cite_note-70) and in the New York newspaper *[The Villager](/wiki/The_Villager_(Manhattan) "The Villager (Manhattan)")*.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Koch also appeared in more than 60 Hollywood films and television shows as himself, including *[Sex and the City](/wiki/Sex_and_the_City "Sex and the City")*, *[Spin City](/wiki/Spin_City "Spin City")*, *[Double Rush](/wiki/Double_Rush "Double Rush")*, a brief cameo in "[The Muppets Take Manhattan](/wiki/The_Muppets_Take_Manhattan "The Muppets Take Manhattan")" and also hosted *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")*.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73) A documentary about his life, *[Koch](/wiki/Koch_(film) "Koch (film)")*, had its world premiere at the [Hamptons International Film Festival](/wiki/Hamptons_International_Film_Festival "Hamptons International Film Festival") on October 8, 2012, and was released theatrically on February 1, 2013 (coincidentally, the day of Koch's death).[[73]](#cite_note-74)

### Political endorsements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Political endorsements")]

After leaving office, Koch frequently endorsed prominent Republican candidates, including [Rudy Giuliani](/wiki/Rudy_Giuliani "Rudy Giuliani")[[74]](#cite_note-75) and [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg")[[75]](#cite_note-76) for mayor, [Al D'Amato](/wiki/Al_D%27Amato "Al D'Amato") for [U.S. Senate](/wiki/U.S._Senate "U.S. Senate"), [Peter T. King](/wiki/Peter_T._King "Peter T. King") for [U.S. House](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), [George Pataki](/wiki/George_Pataki "George Pataki")[[76]](#cite_note-77) for governor, and, in 2004, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") for president.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Koch also endorsed Democrats, including [Eliot Spitzer](/wiki/Eliot_Spitzer "Eliot Spitzer") for governor in the 2006 election. He endorsed [Bill Bradley](/wiki/Bill_Bradley "Bill Bradley") for president in 2000.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

Koch took back his endorsement of Spitzer in the aftermath of the governor's [prostitution scandal](/wiki/Eliot_Spitzer_prostitution_scandal "Eliot Spitzer prostitution scandal"). He said, "At the time the prostitution episode emerged, I commented that nothing could explain his behavior other than the fact that he had a screw loose in his head. Probably several."[[79]](#cite_note-80)

Though Koch supported Giuliani's first mayoral bid, he became opposed to him in January 1996, and began writing a series of columns in the New York *[Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(New_York) "Daily News (New York)")* criticizing Giuliani, most frequently accusing him of being authoritarian and insensitive. In 1999, the columns were compiled into the book *Giuliani: Nasty Man*. He resumed his attacks, and had the book republished, in 2007, after Giuliani announced his candidacy for president. In May 2007, Koch called Giuliani "a control freak" and said that "he wouldn't meet with people he didn't agree with. That's pretty crazy." He also said that Giuliani "was imbued with the thought that if he was right, it was like a God-given right. That's not what we need in a president."[[80]](#cite_note-81)

Koch originally endorsed [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") for president during the 2008 campaign,[[81]](#cite_note-82) then endorsed Democratic nominee [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in the general election. In his endorsement of Obama, Koch wrote that he felt that (unlike in 2004) both candidates would do their best to protect both the United States and Israel from terrorist attacks, but that he agreed with Obama's domestic policies much more and that the idea of Republican vice-presidential nominee [Sarah Palin](/wiki/Sarah_Palin "Sarah Palin") ascending to the presidency "would scare me".[[82]](#cite_note-83) In 2010, he rescinded his support for Obama, saying that Obama could very well harm [American–Israeli relations](/wiki/Israel_%E2%80%93_United_States_relations "Israel – United States relations").[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Koch endorsed Republican [Bob Turner](/wiki/Bob_Turner_(New_York_politician) "Bob Turner (New York politician)") for Congress in 2011 because he "wanted to send a message to Obama to take a stronger position in support of Israel."[[84]](#cite_note-85)

In October 2012, Koch told [Al Sharpton](/wiki/Al_Sharpton "Al Sharpton") that after a conversation with Obama about his position on Israel he was satisfied, and endorsed his reelection.[[85]](#cite_note-86)

Early in 2013, Koch endorsed [Christine Quinn](/wiki/Christine_Quinn "Christine Quinn") in the Democratic primary for that year's [mayoral election](/wiki/New_York_Mayoral_Election,_2013 "New York Mayoral Election, 2013").[[86]](#cite_note-87)

### Other political statements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Other political statements")]

Koch often wrote in defense of Israel and against antisemitism. He also appeared in the documentary *[FahrenHYPE 9/11](/wiki/FahrenHYPE_9/11 "FahrenHYPE 9/11")* defending President Bush and the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan and blasting [Michael Moore](/wiki/Michael_Moore "Michael Moore"). Koch was quoted in the film saying of Moore's film *[Fahrenheit 9/11](/wiki/Fahrenheit_9/11 "Fahrenheit 9/11")*, "It's not a documentary, it's a lie."

Koch praised New York Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo"), saying that he had the right approach in reducing government spending and refraining from raising taxes.[[87]](#cite_note-reason-88)

Koch was an early supporter of the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). In July 2007, Koch wrote that he was "bailing out" of his previous support for that war, due to the failure of the United States' [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") allies, and other [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") countries, to contribute to the war effort. Koch wrote, "I would support our troops remaining in Iraq if our allies were to join us. But they have made it clear they will not." He added that the U.S. must still "prepare for the battles that will take place on American soil by the Islamic forces of terror who are engaged in a war that will be waged by them against Western civilization for at least the next 30 years."[[88]](#cite_note-89)

On April 8, 2010, Koch wrote a piece in *[The Jerusalem Post](/wiki/The_Jerusalem_Post "The Jerusalem Post")* excoriating what he saw as increasing [anti-Catholicism](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism "Anti-Catholicism") in the media, largely made evident by coverage of the priest sex abuse scandals. While denouncing the abuse, Koch wrote, "the procession of articles on the same events are, in my opinion, no longer intended to inform, but simply to castigate." He also wrote that he believed that many in the media, some themselves Catholic, exhibited such anti-Catholicism largely because of their opposition to the Catholic Church's teachings on such issues as abortion, homosexuality, and artificial contraception. He stated that, while he opposed the Church's teaching in all these matters, he firmly believed that the Church had the right to espouse these beliefs and to expect its members to espouse them as well, calling the Church "a force for good in the world, not evil."[[89]](#cite_note-90)

## Wit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Wit")]

A practiced public speaker since his days stumping for [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II"), Koch was well known for his quips and [one-liners](/wiki/One-line_joke "One-line joke").[[90]](#cite_note-NYMag-91) A few include:

* (On the occasion of his primary loss to David Dinkins) "The people have spoken ... and they must be punished."[[90]](#cite_note-NYMag-91)
* "I'm the sort of person who will never [get ulcers](/wiki/Stress_ulcer "Stress ulcer"). Why? Because I say exactly what I think. I'm the sort of person who might give other people ulcers."[[90]](#cite_note-NYMag-91)
* "If you agree with me on nine out of 12 issues, vote for me. If you agree with me on 12 out of 12 issues, see a psychiatrist."[[90]](#cite_note-NYMag-91)

His repeatedly-used catch phrase was "How'm I doing?", said to indicate his awareness of the human appeal of showing interests in the opinions of others.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Washington_Square_Arch-Isabella.jpg/250px-Washington_Square_Arch-Isabella.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Square_Arch-Isabella.jpg)

Prior to his death, Koch lived in an apartment at 2 [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") (the high-rise building to the left of the [Washington Square Arch](/wiki/Washington_Square_Arch "Washington Square Arch"), as seen in the background of a 2016 image of [Washington Square Park](/wiki/Washington_Square_Park "Washington Square Park")).

Koch was a resident of [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village") for most of his adult life. At the time of his death, he lived at 2 Fifth Avenue, overlooking [Washington Square Park](/wiki/Washington_Square_Park "Washington Square Park").[[92]](#cite_note-Andrew_Berman-93)

### Sexuality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sexuality")]

Koch never married and had no children.[[93]](#cite_note-94) Rumors about his [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation") became an issue in the 1977 mayoral election with the appearance of placards and posters with the slogan "Vote for Cuomo, Not the Homo." Koch denounced the attack, and the Cuomo campaign disavowed them.[[94]](#cite_note-95) [Mario](/wiki/Mario_Cuomo "Mario Cuomo") and [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") denied any involvement, but Koch and others did not believe them.[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In 1989, Koch was interviewed about a book he had coauthored with Cardinal [John J. O'Connor](/wiki/John_Joseph_O%27Connor_(cardinal) "John Joseph O'Connor (cardinal)"). When the interviewer asked Koch to clarify his views on homosexuality relative to those of O'Connor, Koch responded, "I happen to believe that there's nothing wrong with homosexuality. It's whatever God made you. It happens that I'm a heterosexual."[[97]](#cite_note-98) He once told *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine, "Listen, there's no question that some New Yorkers think I'm gay, and voted for me nevertheless. The vast majority don't care, and others don't think I am. And I don't give a shit either way!"[[7]](#cite_note-NYT22-8)[[98]](#cite_note-CNNKochobit-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) He was frequently accompanied at political functions by his friend [Bess Myerson](/wiki/Bess_Myerson "Bess Myerson"),[[100]](#cite_note-Dillon-101) who also acted as co-chair of his 1977 election campaign.[[101]](#cite_note-Berman-102)

In a 2022 article, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* confirmed Koch's homosexuality based on interviews with close confidants. The *Times* reported that Koch disclosed his sexuality only to friends who were also gay. His only known long-term relationship was with health care consultant Richard W. Nathan. Koch quickly ended the relationship after being elected mayor. In the 1980s, activist and writer [Larry Kramer](/wiki/Larry_Kramer "Larry Kramer"), who was critical of Koch's handling of the [AIDS epidemic](/wiki/HIV/AIDS_in_the_United_States "HIV/AIDS in the United States"), unsuccessfully attempted to [out](/wiki/Outing "Outing") him after learning of the relationship with Nathan. This influenced Koch's insistence on remaining closeted for the rest of his life, as he did not "want to give activists like Mr. Kramer the satisfaction of seeing him come out, after they had tried so hard to see him outed". In old age, Koch attempted to find a male romantic partner. He was unsuccessful, and he considered his lack of a partner the only pursuit of his life that he failed to accomplish.[[7]](#cite_note-NYT22-8)

### Health, death, and funeral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ed_Koch&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Health, death, and funeral")]

Koch had a mild stroke in 1987, but was able to resume his duties as mayor within about a week.[[7]](#cite_note-NYT22-8)[[102]](#cite_note-103)

After his mayoralty, Koch experienced further health problems that included problems with his heart. He was frequently hospitalized in the final months of his life.[[103]](#cite_note-McFadden-104)[[104]](#cite_note-BBC_death-105) On January 31, 2013, he was admitted to [Columbia University Irving Medical Center](/wiki/Columbia_University_Irving_Medical_Center "Columbia University Irving Medical Center") in Manhattan due to fatigue, where he died from heart failure at 2 a.m. the next day, aged 88.[[98]](#cite_note-CNNKochobit-99)[[103]](#cite_note-McFadden-104) His funeral took place on February 4, 2013, at [Temple Emanu-El](/wiki/Congregation_Emanu-El_of_New_York "Congregation Emanu-El of New York"), a [Reform Jewish](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") congregation in Manhattan.[[105]](#cite_note-hufpo130213-106) Former president [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") addressed the congregation, serving as President Obama's representative. Other speakers included then-mayor Michael Bloomberg. [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") helicopters gave a fly-over at the service.[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[105]](#cite_note-hufpo130213-106)

Koch purchased a burial plot in [Trinity Church Cemetery](/wiki/Trinity_Church_Cemetery "Trinity Church Cemetery") in April 2008 so that he could be buried in Manhattan. It is the only graveyard in the borough that accepts new burials. He chose to put the last words of the late journalist [Daniel Pearl](/wiki/Daniel_Pearl "Daniel Pearl") on his tombstone: "My father is Jewish, my mother is Jewish, I am Jewish."[[107]](#cite_note-108)
